返回本批次

编程曾经是免费的

Programming Used to Be Free

purplesyringa.moe 作者 yeputons 分数 69 评论 71 2026/04/13 14:24
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 2026 年 4 月 13 日龙虾黑客新闻 Mythos（据称能够发现大量 0-day 的私人法学硕士）的出现让人们担心无法获得强大的工具。这似乎是主流话语的一个转折点，它激励我完成我一直想写的思考文章。我对法学硕士有一个相关的、亲密的担忧。我们需要明确的是，这并不是反人工智能人群的常见批评，例如道德或质量。虽然我也有一些保留意见，但坦率地说，这并不是让我最焦虑的事情。我的目的是让这篇文章发人深省，即使你对这个主题的看法与我完全不同。背景故事我将从一些个人的开始。感谢我的父亲，我在 2010 年代初作为一个孩子开始编程。他不是一名软件开发人员，所以他坚持使用他使用和理解的技术：QBasic。它是 35 年前用于 BASIC 的 MS-DOS IDE，这正是您对此类旧软件的期望：缓慢的解释器、80x25 文本模式输出、图形模式下的 16（！）种颜色以及蓝底白字编辑器。我没有通过 NTVDM 或 DOSBox（当时常见的模拟器）在 Windows 7 笔记本电脑上运行它。不，这是一台真正符合时代要求的个人电脑。我没有自己的照片，但这里有一张库存图片，只是为了让大家感受一下：当时我不太懂英语，而且也没有内置文档，所以我必须通过反复试验来学习。从头开始重做仍然困扰着我。我告诉你这些并不是为了吹牛或乞求同情——我只是在提供背景，说明为什么我在谈论计算的历史时感到很舒服，尽管我还年轻而且天真。尽管我没有经历过它，但我听到了一些故事，尽管我的堆栈已经现代化，但我经常发现自己在研究逆向计算。教育当我终于接触到 Windows PC 时，我在偶然找到一本自学书籍后开始学习 PHP。我很快就找到了 php.net 并转向在线文档。我学会了使用文件系统，设置 Apache，使用 C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\etc\hosts。它从那里滚雪球般越滚越大。你几乎不需要任何资源就可以学到很多东西。当然，我需要互联网接入，但我使用的是过时的 Windows XP 机器（我相信）Pentium II 就可以了。后来，我从PVS-Studio博客和大量在线教程中学习了C++语法。提供免费知识的地方的数量怎么强调也不为过。 htmlbook.ru 和 javascript.ru 帮助我学习了 webdev，仅举几例。别忘了可汗学院！虽然那时我换了一台功能更强大的笔记本电脑，但我使用它的时间超出了它的预期寿命，直到它无法跟上 Windows 的速度。由于优化，我能够在这段时间使用浏览器、IDE 和其他免费工具。唯一没有响应的程序是 VS Code，但它很容易替换。我非常感谢能够访问这么多信息和工具。正是无数人的努力，尽最大努力免费提供高质量的编译器和教程，才让我最终成为了人们敬仰的人。大型机时代并不总是这样。曾经有一段时间，没有 GCC，没有 Linux，也没有 VS Code。一些主要供应商提供了专有的、昂贵的编译器和系统。很难找到确切的价格，但 1990 年左右，Watcom C/C++（被 DOOM 等项目使用）售价为 1000 美元，AT&T UNIX 的源代码售价为 10,000 美元。 BSD/386 的 1000 美元成本被认为非常便宜。虽然这让核心基础设施的开发人员得到了报酬，但这意味着只有大型技术公司和大学才能负担得起最先进的软件——业余爱好者只能靠自己了。随着 20 世纪 80 年代个人电脑的普及，如 Amiga、ZX Spectrum 和 Commodore 64（千字节，而不是位），人们能够开发自己的程序（通常是游戏），但仅限于 BASIC 和汇编，这要么是限制性的，要么需要微妙的、非广泛的知识。 尽管人们通过运动鞋网或 LAN 聚会分享业余爱好者和盗版专有软件是很常见的，但这种文化并没有看到人们在大型开源软件系统稍后再说。也许是因为人们扮演公司，添加有趣的免费软件许可证并实施版权保护以获取乐趣；或者也许每个人都试图以此谋生。自由软件运动是扭转潮流的驱动力。提供免费的——无论是自由还是免费啤酒——C编译器、IDE、操作系统内核和用户空间充当了催化剂，为开源软件塑造我们今天生活的世界铺平了道路。隐性成本我非常感谢 FOSS 社区及其周围的人，他们让我能够不受限制地访问信息和软件，因为这就是让我首先进入这个职业的原因。这并不是感谢免费试用 – 我需要能够在一个月后继续学习。这不是学生计划 – 我是一个没有代理的孩子，无法提交任何确认文件或自掏腰包。这不是免费计划 – 我已经受到地位和知识差距的限制，进一步的限制只会加剧问题。我将 GitHub Pages、GitLab CI/CD 和 Heroku 组合在一起来实现服务器端逻辑。我使用去中心化网络。 0 美元和 1 美元之间的区别并不在于“免费”与“便宜”。在我的情况下，它是“可能”与“无法实现”。LLMs这让我想到了LLMs。当在本地运行时，它们的性能直接随着计算能力的变化而变化。在低端设备上运行 GCC 可能需要五分钟而不是两分钟，但如果您没有 GPU 或足够的 RAM，LLM 将直接无法使用。即使是最简单的编码代理也需要比普通人更多的硬件。对于封闭权重的法学硕士，你必须尝试不同的模型和公司，同时为每个模型和公司付费（关于哪个模型最好的意见每个月都会变化）。最简单的解决方案就是把它变成你雇主的问题：以前足够的廉价模型被切除，免费使用受到高度限制。这对任何人来说都不会感到震惊：法学硕士的运行和维护成本很高。它仍然很糟糕。我不知道兼顾法学硕士是否应该成为软件开发的中心任务。我怀疑有人真正知道。但这个行业正在发生变化，LLM支持的编程可能会以某种形式继续成为其中的主要部分。无论LLM优先的工作流程是什么样子，它们都不会像过去的方法那样容易理解。这些功能不仅对公司和计算机学生有效，而且对那些无法提交教育计划文档的人、欠发达国家的开发人员和小型团队也有效。中学生可以在家庭 iPad 上学习 Python。他们无法学习 vivicoding。结论因此，这可能会让计算回归 20 世纪 70 年代的富豪统治，这让我很困扰。如果你没有经历过那个时代，你很容易认为那段时期是民间传说中的计算黄金时代，但它也是昂贵的、不民主的和限制性的。经济不稳定、“害怕错过”，再加上昂贵的订阅费用、快节奏和供应商锁定，可能会让新的实践在公司和大学等机构之外变得棘手。你可能买得起——那些买不起的人呢？我知道你们中的一些人可以用有限的资源、软件或硬件来凑合。尽管如此，到目前为止，我们在此过程中学到的经验教训还是有用的。但在以法学硕士为中心的编程中，它们很容易变得毫无价值。令我难过的是，我不得不怀疑是否没有其他人能够走我走过的路。

Original Summary

  1. April 13, 2026 Lobsters Hacker NewsThe appearance of Mythos – a private LLM allegedly capable of finding a multitude of 0-days – has made people concerned about being denied powerful tools. This seems to be a turning point in the mainstream discourse, and it motivated me to complete the think piece I’ve been meaning to write for a while.I have a related, intimate worry regarding LLMs. Just so that we’re clear, it’s not a common critique from the anti-AI crowd, like ethics or quality. While I share some reservations, frankly it’s not what gives me the most angst. My intent is to make this post thought-provoking even if your beliefs on this topic entirely differ from mine.BackstoryI’m going to start a little personal.I started programming as a child in the beginning of 2010s, thanks to my dad. He didn’t work as a software developer, so he insisted on using the technology he used and understood: QBasic. It’s an MS-DOS IDE for BASIC back from 35 years ago, and it was what you’d expect from such old software: a slow interpreter, 80x25 text mode output, 16 (!) colors in graphical mode, and a white-on-blue-background editor.I didn’t run it on a Windows 7 laptop via NTVDM or DOSBox – common emulators of that time. No, it was an actual epoch-appropriate PC. I don’t have photos of my own, but here’s a stock picture just to get the feel across:I didn’t know English much at the time, and there wasn’t built-in documentation regardless, so I had to learn by trial and error. Redo from start still haunts me.I’m not telling you this to brag or beg for sympathy – I’m giving context for why I feel comfortable talking about the history of computing despite my young age and naivety. Even though I didn’t live through it, I heard tales, and I often find myself researching retrocomputing despite having modernized my stack.EducationWhen I finally got access to a Windows PC, I started learning PHP after finding a self-teach book by accident. I found php.net pretty soon and switched to online documentation. I learned to work with the filesystem, set up Apache, played around with C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\etc\hosts. It snowballed from there.You could learn a lot of stuff with barely any resources. I needed Internet access, sure, but I used an outdated Windows XP machine with (I believe) Pentium II just fine. Later, I learned C++ syntax from the PVS-Studio blog and a multitude of online tutorials.The number of places offering free knowledge cannot be overstated. htmlbook.ru and javascript.ru helped me learn webdev, to name a few. And don’t forget Khan Academy!Although I switched to a somewhat more powerful laptop by that point, I used it beyond its intended lifetime until it couldn’t keep up with Windows. I was able to use browsers, IDEs, and other free tools during all that time thanks to optimization. The only program that wasn’t responsive was VS Code, but it was easy to replace.I immensely appreciate having access to this much information and tools. It was the work of countless people, doing their best to provide high-quality compilers and tutorials for free that allowed me to eventually become a person people look up to.The mainframe ageIt hasn’t always been that way.There was a time when there was no GCC, no Linux, and no VS Code. There were proprietary, expensive compilers and systems provided by a few major vendors. It’s difficult to find the exact prices, but around 1990, Watcom C/C++ (used by DOOM among other projects) cost $1000, and the source for AT&T UNIX cost $10’000. The $1000 cost of BSD/386 was considered incredibly cheap.While this got the developers of core infrastructure paid, it meant that only large technical companies and universities could afford state-of-the-art software – hobbyists were left on their own. With the proliferation of personal computers in 1980s, like Amiga, ZX Spectrum, and Commodore 64 (kilobytes, not bits), people were able to develop their own programs (usually games), but only in BASIC and assembly, which was either limiting or required subtle, non-widespread knowledge.Even though it was common for people to share hobbyist and pirated proprietary software via sneakernet or at LAN parties, the culture didn’t see people collaborating on large open-source software systems until later. Perhaps it was because people cosplayed corporations, adding amusing freeware licenses and implementing copy protection for fun; or perhaps everyone tried to make a living out of it.The Free Software movement was the driving force behind turning the tide. Providing a free – both as in freedom and as in free beer – C compiler, IDE, and OS kernel and userspace acted as a catalyst, paving the way for open-source software to shape the world we live in today.Hidden costI’m deeply grateful to the FOSS community and the people around it for enabling unfettered access to information and software, because that’s what allowed me to get into this profession in the first place.It wasn’t thanks to programs or services with free trials – I needed to be able to keep learning after a month has passed.It wasn’t thanks to student plans – I was a child without agency who couldn’t submit any confirmational documentation or pay out of pocket.It wasn’t thanks to free plans – I was already limited by status and knowledge gaps, and further restrictions would only exacerbate the issue.I hacked together GitHub Pages, GitLab CI/CD, and Heroku to implement server-side logic. I used decentralized networks. The difference between $0 and $1 wasn’t “free” vs “cheap”. In my circumstances, it was “possible” vs “unachievable”.LLMsWhich brings me to LLMs.When running locally, their performance directly scales with computing power. Running GCC on a low-end device might take five minutes instead of two, but LLMs become straight up unusable if you don’t have a GPU or enough RAM. Even the simplest coding agent requires more hardware than an average person has.With closed-weight LLMs, you have to play around with different models and companies, paying for each one in the meantime (the opinion on which one’s the best changes every month). The simplest solution is to make it your employer’s problem: previously adequate cheap models are lobotomized and free usage is highly limited.It’s not a shock to anyone: LLMs are expensive to run and maintain. It still sucks.I don’t know if juggling LLMs should be a central task of software development. I doubt anyone truly knows. But the industry is changing, and LLM-enabled programming will likely remain a major part of it in some shape or form.And whatever LLM-first workflows will look like, they won’t be nearly as accessible as the approaches of yesteryear. Those functioned well not only for companies and compsci students, but also for those who couldn’t submit documentation for an educational plan, developers in underdeveloped countries, and tiny teams.A middle schooler can learn Python on a family iPad. They can’t learn vibecoding.ConclusionAnd so it bothers me that this might regress computing back to the plutocracy of 1970s. It’s easy to think of that time as the golden age of computing from folklore if you didn’t live through it, but it was also expensive, undemocratic, and limiting.Economical instability, FOMO combined with costly subscriptions, fast pacing, and vendor lock-in can make new practices intractable outside of institutions, like firms and universities. You might afford it – what about those who can’t?I know some of you can relate to making do with limited resources, software, or hardware. So far, the lessons we learned along that way have been useful despite that. But they can easily become worthless in LLM-centric programming. And it saddens me that I have to wonder if no one else will be able to walk the road I did.

评论（默认中文）

roxolotl 2026/04/13 21:13
我有理由相信这是反对法学硕士的最佳论据。这与 Open 以 OpenAI 的名字命名的原因相同。人们普遍认为，集中这些工具的所有权将改变世界。这就是投资如此之高的原因。如果权力和财富没有集中到这些人工智能实验室，那么投资就不值得。这意味着我们必须问自己是否想要这样。有很多未来包括法学硕士但不包括集中化，但它们需要偏离我们当前的轨迹。也不能保证编程和计算会像今天一样免费。
I’m reasonably convinced this is the best argument against LLMs. It’s the same reason Open is in OpenAI’s name. The understanding that centralizing the ownership of these tools is going to transform the world is widespread. That’s why the investment is so high. If power and wealth isn’t concentrated into these AI labs the investment isn’t worth it. Which means we have to ask ourselves if we want that. There’s plenty of futures which include LLMs and don’t include the centralization but they require a departure from our current trajectory. There was also no guarantee that programming and computing would become free like it is today.
在 HN 中查看
yabutlivnWoods 2026/04/14 05:00
最好的反对理由是，它们只是支撑数据中心公司的另一个计划。

使用具有 Linux 内核和 tect 编辑器同等知识的法学硕士？或者 git 克隆它们。

这是另一个被卖给不了解情况的政客和年长投资者的国家管理计划。大型科技公司 100% 依赖于虐待老年人。

The best argument against is they're just another scheme to prop up data center companies.

Use an LLM with the equivalent knowledge of Linux kernel and tect editor? Or git clone them.

It's another state management scheme being sold to politicians and elder investors who don't know any better. Big tech 100% relies on elder abuse.

在 HN 中查看
estimator7292 2026/04/14 04:19
> 有很多未来包括法学硕士但不包括集中化，但它们需要偏离我们当前的轨迹。

我认为这根本不是真的。很明显，本地模型是代理编码的未来，每个人都在朝着这个目标迈进。

越来越明显的是，当前的模型是 很多 比他们真正需要的更大。新的研究表明，大多数变压器模型可以显着缩小，但性能仍然相同。

我们肯定还没有做到这一点，但是在单个消费者 GPU 上运行的模型正在以相当快的速度变得更好。模型尺寸不断减小，效率不断提高，计算速度越来越快、成本越来越低。

我真的看不到未来会出现巨大的数据中心 仅有的 运行编码代理的方式。大型模型可能会继续保持更高的性能，但与本地模型之间的差距正在迅速缩小。

> There's plenty of futures which include LLMs and don’t include the centralization but they require a departure from our current trajectory.

I don't think that's true at all. It's pretty clear that local models are the future of agentic coding, and everyone's been moving towards that goal.

It's also becoming clear that current models are much bigger than they really need to be. New research indicates that most transformer models can be shrunk significantly and still perform the same.

We definitely aren't there yet, but models that run on a single consumer GPU are getting better at a pretty fast pace. Model size keeps going down, efficiency keeps going up, and compute keeps getting faster and cheaper.

I really don't see a future where enormous datacenters are the only way to run a coding agent. Huge models might continue to be more performant, but the gap between that and a local model is closing quick.

在 HN 中查看
h05sz487b 2026/04/14 00:02
我不知道，曾经有 IDE 供应商向企业出售产品并提供用于教育目的的免费赠品。今后，老牌厂商将提供免费产品，以及您可以在本地运行的 OSS 模型。目前，这在中学生可能使用的现有硬件上当然不太令人愉快，在 MacBook Neo 中添加更多 RAM，情况可能会改变。
I don't know, there used to be IDE vendors that sold stuff to enterprises and offered freebies for educational purposes. Down the line there will be free offerings by the established players as well as OSS models you can run locally. Right now this is of course not enjoyable on existing hardware that a middle schooler might be using, put a bit more RAM into the MacBook Neo and this might change.
在 HN 中查看
tnelsond4 2026/04/13 16:48
即使在过去，如果您想学习如何编写最好的代码，也必须购买编程书籍和课程。那不是免费的。这实际上与法学硕士没什么不同，你可以在没有它们的情况下编码，但它们是一个很好的资源，可以在你遇到困难时帮助你。您可以使用 10 亿个免费的法学硕士、Grok、duck.ai 等。您不需要钱或订阅 vivi 代码。
Even back in the day you had to buy programming books and courses if you wanted to learn how to make the best code. That wasn't free. It's really not all that different from LLMs, you can code without them, but they're a good resource to help you when you're stuck. There's a billion free LLMs you can use, Grok, duck.ai, etc. you don't need money or a subscription to vibe code.
在 HN 中查看
compass_copium 2026/04/13 23:43
这是作者和帖子的一部分直接提到的？在海湾合作委员会等出现之前，工具非常昂贵，并且互联网提供了优秀的免费指南。
This is directly addressed by the author and part of the post? Tools were very expensive until gcc etc., and the internet made excellent free guides available.
在 HN 中查看
walljm 2026/04/13 23:52
并且有免费模型可用。以及运行它们的免费方式...
and there are free models available. and free ways to run them...
在 HN 中查看
mghackerlady 2026/04/13 23:54
他们还解决了这个问题，并讨论了竞争模型如何无法在大多数人拥有的较弱硬件上运行
they also addressed this and talked about how competitive models can't run on the weaker hardware most people have
在 HN 中查看
walljm 2026/04/14 02:37
大多数可用服务（anthropic、google、openai、xai、deepseek）都有免费套餐。你不能广泛使用它，并且必须等待......但它就在那里。

编程总是有门的……今天也不例外。可以说，现在的免费选择比我刚来时要多得多。

most of the available services (anthropic, google, openai, xai, deepseek) have a free tier. you can't use it extensively, and have to wait... but its there.

programming has always gates... today is no different. arguably, there are quite a lot more free options than there were when i was coming up.

在 HN 中查看
AndrewKemendo 2026/04/14 00:01
在台式计算机出现之前，您必须实际去实验室工作才能进行任何编程，这需要大量的教育和社交机会

有什么意义？

编写部署和交付软件从未如此容易

就像作者一样，我也是自学的，而且得到的帮助要少得多，因为我什至没有父母指导我完成它

And prior to the desktop computer, you had to actually go work at a laboratory in order to do any programming whatsoever, which required significant amounts of educational and social access

What’s the point?

Writing deploying and delivering software has never been as accessible as it has ever been

Much like the author I learned on my own too and with a lot less help because I didn’t have a parent even guiding me through it

在 HN 中查看
mghackerlady 2026/04/14 00:15
这就是本文的主题，回到这个话题是一件坏事
that is literally what this article is about, how returning to that is a bad thing
在 HN 中查看
AndrewKemendo 2026/04/14 01:49
但这并没有受到威胁，我不确定为什么人们认为它是

没有一个论点偶然地表明知识较少或选择较少。

这是锁定时间最少的时期，人工智能获得的越好，它将成为锁定更少的选项，因为你可以在自己的硬件上自己构建和运行所有内容

从字面上看，此时任何人都可以在少数退役服务器和旧笔记本电脑上运行相当于 2000 年整个数据中心的数据。

But that is not under threat and I’m not sure why people think it is

None of the arguments demonstrate even accidentally that there is LESS knowledge or fewer options.

This is the least locked in period and the better AI gets it will be an option to be even less locked in because you can just build and run everything yourself on your own hardware

Literally anyone can run the equivalent of an entire datacenter from 2000 on a handful of retired servers and old laptops at this point.

在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:18
...这需要相当昂贵的计算机。
...that require fairly expensive computers.

coldtea 2026/04/14 01:07
>即使在过去，如果您想学习如何编写最好的代码，也必须购买编程书籍和课程。那不是免费的

“即使在灭绝级流星袭击俄亥俄州之前，一直都有微小的流星袭击地球，这也不是那么安全”。

>Even back in the day you had to buy programming books and courses if you wanted to learn how to make the best code. That wasn't free

"Even before the extinction level meteor hit Ohio, there were tiny meteors hitting Earth all the time, it wasn't that safe either".

在 HN 中查看
purplesyringa 2026/04/13 21:24
您仍然可以在没有法学硕士的情况下编写代码，就像您可以在没有现代 IDE 的情况下编写代码一样，或者使用 C 和汇编而不是高级语言。但在此过程中学到的技能之间存在显着差异，我认为这会抑制向上流动。
You can still write code without LLMs, much like you can write code without modern IDEs, or use C and assembly instead of higher-level languages. But there are significant differences between the skills you learn in the process, which I believe inhibits upward mobility.
在 HN 中查看
tincholio 2026/04/13 22:47
好吧，早在那时，开发工具在很大程度上也不是免费的。
Well, way back in the day, dev tools weren't free, either, for the most part.
在 HN 中查看
WalterBright 2026/04/13 23:32
在 80 年代，一个好的编译器要花费几百美元。无情的竞争将价格压低至零。
In the 80s, a good compiler would cost several hundred dollars. Relentless competition pushed the prices down to zero.
在 HN 中查看
bombcar 2026/04/13 23:54
有些人在编译器通常比其运行的计算机更昂贵时开始玩计算机，而那些追随者可以免费下载整个“Unix”系统和工具链。

整个行业和大型公司的存在都是为了工具和工具，而现在这些工具和工具被认为是免费的和赌注。哎呀，操作系统过去要花钱，而且根本没有什么东西！

There are those who started playing with computers when compilers were often more expensive than the computer they ran on, and those who came after you could download an entire "Unix" system and toolchain for free.

Entire industries and massive companies existed for tools and tooling that is now considered free and table-stakes. Heck, an operating system used to cost money and didn't come with much at all!

在 HN 中查看
WalterBright 2026/04/14 12:05
PC-DOS 起价为 40 美元。由于 IBM PC 的价格约为 3000 美元，因此 40 美元或多或少是免费的。
PC-DOS started out at $40. Since the IBM PC cost about $3000, the $40 was more or less free.
在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:19
随着信息的分发成本接近于零。
Along with distribution costs for information going to near zero.
在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:17
我不确定这有多真实。 互联网上有大量免费信息可供学习编码。
I'm not sure how true that is. There was copious free information on the internet to learn about coding.
在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/14 00:35
我很幸运，在互联网充满免费学习资源的时代长大，但在那之前有一段时间，你确实需要实体书来超越基础知识。

我记得和比我年长几十年的人交谈时，当他们谈到必须购买编译器时我感到很困惑。

I was fortunate to grow up when the internet was full of free learning resources, but there was a time just before that when you really did need physical books to get beyond the basics.

I remember talking to people a couple decades older than me and being confused when they talked about having to buy compilers, too.

在 HN 中查看
zajio1am 2026/04/14 00:06
如果您不考虑时间成本，编程是免费的。如果你考虑一下，它比人工智能相关的成本要高得多。即使考虑到与人工智能相关的成本，它仍然比大多数其他工程专业便宜得多，而物理实现的成本要高出几个数量级。
Programming is free if you do not consider price of your time. If you consider it, it is much higher than AI-associated costs. And even with AI-associated costs, it is still much cheaper than most other engineering professions, where physical realization is orders of magnitude more costly.
在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:14
当然啦。 这篇文章讲述了作者作为一个没有钱但有充足时间的年轻人的经历。

这正是需要广泛使用法学硕士的编程文化可能会排除的人。

Well of course. The article is about the author's experience of being a young person with no money but plenty of time.

This is exactly the kind of person that could be excluded by a programming culture that requires extensive use of LLMs.

在 HN 中查看
discreteevent 2026/04/14 02:07
如果你是一个有足够时间的年轻人，那么你花时间的最好方式就是学习没有人工智能的编程——无论你有没有钱。
If you were a young person with plenty of time, the best way you could spend it would be learning to program without AI- whether you have money or not.
在 HN 中查看
tracker1 2026/04/14 02:03
不过，法学硕士并不是必需的……如果你正在学习，那么没有这些东西你可能会过得更好。 .com 泡沫破裂后，我陷入了困境，住在朋友正在装修的房子里，有一段时间没有互联网接入……我从一本厚厚的书和 beta 命令行编译器中学习了 C#……多年来，我比同龄人更了解这门语言和工具。

你无法通过法学硕士获得那样的深度……因为你通常不会深入研究……就此而言，如果你是氛围编码，你甚至会进一步远离事情如何完成的细节，无论是好是坏。

LLMs aren't a requirement though.. and if you're learning, you're probably better off without the things. I was pretty down and out after the .com bubble burst and was staying in a house a friend was renovating without internet access for a while... I learned C# from a big fat book and the beta command line compiler... for years, I knew the language and tools better than my peers.

You can't get that level of depth with an LLM... because you generally won't be digging in... for that matter, if you're vibe coding, you're even further removed from the details of how things are being done for better or worse.

在 HN 中查看
kibwen 2026/04/14 00:18
LLM 提供商感兴趣的是利润最大化，而不是最小化成本。提供商的最终目标，以及他们拥有数万亿美元估值的原因，是因为目标是占领市场，然后提高价格，以获取您通过使用它们可能节省的任何时间的价值。换句话说，如果使用 LLM 为您节省的时间达到每小时 100 美元，那么他们的目标是最终向您收取每小时 99.99 美元的使用费。
LLM providers are interested in maximizing their profits, not minimizing your costs. The eventual goal of the providers, and the reason that they have trillion-dollar valuations, is because the objective is to capture the market and then increase the price to capture the value of any time you may be saving by using them. In other words, if your time savings amounts to $100 per hour by using LLMs, their goal is to eventually charge you $99.99 per hour for the privilege of using them.
在 HN 中查看
mzelling 2026/04/14 00:22
一个有趣的副作用可能是，只有那些无法使用法学硕士的人才会在未来学习如何编程，因为视频编码不会教你基础知识。

我知道你在想什么——当计算器出现时，被迫在头脑中计算并不是一个优势。但法学硕士不同：计算器是心算的严格改进的替代品，而法学硕士只是一个近似的解决方案——考虑到上下文管理、解释意图等方面的微妙挑战，法学硕士是否会成为一个完美的解决方案还远不清楚。

An interesting side effect might be that only people locked out from using LLMs will learn how to program in the future, as vide coding doesn't teach you the fundamentals.

I know what you're thinking — when the calculator came about, being forced to compute in your head wasn't an advantage. But LLMs are different: a calculator is a strictly improved substitute for mental arithmetic, whereas an LLM is only an approximate solution — and it is far from clear whether LLMs will ever become a perfect solution, given the nuanced challenges around context management, interpreting intent, etc.

在 HN 中查看
1718627440 2026/04/14 12:01
> 当计算器出现时，被迫在头脑中计算并不是一个优势。

我不确定这是否属实，因为当教育工作者希望你学习如何计算时，你就被计算器“拒之门外”。 在学习了基本算术之后你才能使用计算器，并且当你应该学习微积分时你不会使用 CAS。

> when the calculator came about, being forced to compute in your head wasn't an advantage.

I'm not sure, whether that is true, because when educators want you to learn how to compute you are "locked out" of calculators. You don't get to use a calculator until after you learned basic arithmetic and you won't use a CAS when you are supposed to learn calculus.

在 HN 中查看
professoretc 2026/04/14 06:11
> 一个有趣的副作用可能是，只有那些无法使用法学硕士的人才会在未来学习如何编程，因为视频编码不会教你基础知识。

在思考/与法学硕士一起工作时，我不止一次地想起阿西莫夫的短篇小说《职业》（http://employees.oneonta.edu/blechmjb/JBpages/m360/Professio...）。在其中，没有人去上学：信息只是被输入到你的大脑中。当你还是个孩子的时候，你会得到一些基础知识，然后是你职业生涯的所有专业信息（这是根据你的大脑布局最适合的为你选择的）。

主角是众多人中的一员 不能 获取第二个转储；他的大脑不正常，所以他被送到弱智者之家，与其他必须学习老式方法的人在一起。

通过各种冒险，他最终意识到每个被“录音”的人都是 无法 根本不学习新材料。他的弱智者之家实际上是一所高等研究院，是少数几个负责 全部 维持人类进步的发明和创造。

> An interesting side effect might be that only people locked out from using LLMs will learn how to program in the future, as vide coding doesn't teach you the fundamentals.

While thinking about/working with LLMs, I've been reminded more than once of Asimov's short story Profession (http://employees.oneonta.edu/blechmjb/JBpages/m360/Professio...). In it, no one goes to school: information is just dumped into your brain. You get an initial dump of the basics when you're a kid, and then later all the specialty information for your career (which is chosen for you, based on what your brain layout is most suited to).

The protagonist is one of a number of people who can't get the second dump; his brain just isn't wired right, so he's sent to a Home for the Feeble Minded to be with other people who have to learn the old-fashioned way.

Through various adventures he eventually realizes that everyone who was "taped" is incapable of learning new material at all. His Home for the Feeble Minded is in fact an Institute of Higher Studies, one of only a handful, which are responsible for all the invention and creation that sustains human progress.

在 HN 中查看
godshatter 2026/04/14 00:43
> 一个有趣的副作用可能是，只有那些无法使用法学硕士的人才会在未来学习如何编程，因为视频编码不会教你基础知识。

这对我来说是奇怪的部分。我是那些我认为在 HN 上很常见的人之一 - 很长一段时间以来，我一直在个人项目上享受编码的乐趣，大约是在 1978 年 iirc 的某个地方。在我工作的地方，我们开始涉足人工智能和振动编码，但我不是它的忠实粉丝。即使在我无聊的工作中，实际编码也是我最喜欢的部分。所以我采取了不同的策略。我一直在鼓励克劳德教我如何做事，效果非常好。首先提供一些基本信息，然后根据需要提出具体问题，但我正在做这项工作。我在提高工作效率的同时仍然学习新事物并享受乐趣。对我来说双赢。

> An interesting side effect might be that only people locked out from using LLMs will learn how to program in the future, as vide coding doesn't teach you the fundamentals.

This is the strange part for me. I'm one of those people that I assume are really common here on HN - I've been having fun coding on personal projects for a long time, somewhere circa 1978 iirc for me. Where I work we're starting to dip our toes into AI and vibecoding and I'm not a big fan. Even in my boring job the actual coding is the part I like the most. So I've taken a different tack. I've been prompting Claude to teach me how to do things, and that has worked out really well. Some basic info to start with, specific questions as needed, but I'm doing the work. I'm improving my productivity while still learning new things and having fun. Win-win for me.

在 HN 中查看
queenkjuul 2026/04/14 04:12
Gemini 一直在教我嵌入式 Linux，去年 ChatGPT 教我 C#。请注意所有免费套餐。但我正在做这项工作，提出问题比挖掘邮件列表和源代码更快。

但在工作中，快速行动的压力太大了，所以这些天我让 Claude Code 做更多的工作（远不及大多数，但我找到了我可以信任的东西）。

鉴于模型的不可预测性和定价的不透明性，我认为我自己无法处理付费计划。

Gemini has been teaching me embedded Linux, and last year ChatGPT taught me C#. All on the free tiers mind you. But I'm doing the work, it's just faster to ask questions than to dig through mailing lists and source code.

At work though, the pressure to move fast is too high, so I'm letting Claude Code so more work these days (nowhere near the majority, but I've found things i can trust it with).

I don't think i could deal with a paid plan myself given how unpredictable the models are and opaque the pricing is.

在 HN 中查看
godshatter 2026/04/14 06:15
我开始在家里这样做，但进行网络搜索的本能仍然存在。我只在工作中使用 Claude Code，因为他们要付费，所以为什么不使用它呢。我想到目前为止，我在任何一天都使用了大概 5% 的代币。我需要选择一个免费的人工智能，并让它成为我想要学习的人工智能导师。

一旦我在工作中构建了一些东西，我可能会要求克劳德·科德（Claude Code）寻找我所写内容的问题，但我们还没有被强迫进入人工智能编码，尽管不祥之兆。我主要是在寻找方法来扩展我在当前限制下可以做的事情，并保持理智。

I'm starting to do this at home, but the instinct to just do a web search is still there. I'm only using Claude Code at work because they are paying for it, so why not use it. I think I've used maybe 5% of my tokens for any given day so far. I need to pick a free AI and make it my goto AI mentor for what I want to learn.

Once I build a few things at work I'll probably be asking Claude Code to look for problems with what I've written, but we're not being pushed too hard to get into AI coding yet, though the writing is on the wall. I'm mostly looking for ways to expand what I can do within our current constraints, and keep my sanity.

在 HN 中查看
tracker1 2026/04/14 01:59
有一个小问题...Basic 也不是真正免费的。至少不是 QB45 等。它不是超级贵，但不是免费的...还值得一提的是，早在 80 年代和 90 年代初，计算机本身的成本与许多人当时驾驶的汽车一样多或更高。 我记得在 1993 年看到过一台二手 XT，价格约为 200 美元左右，这已经足够便宜了，但最先进的是 80486 DX2 66...

今天，你可以以大约 20 美元/月的价格获得 Claude Code 或 Codex 的入门级子程序……虽然这在世界某些地方可能非常昂贵，但它并不像 90 年代初期的单一最先进的编译器或开发工具那么糟糕，在下一版本发布之前的一两年内。 更不用说像 MSDN 订阅这样的东西了。

One niggle... Basic wasn't really free either. At least not QB45, etc. It's wasn't super expensive, but it wasn't free... Also worth a mention is computers themselves back in the 80's and early 90's costs as much or more than the cars a lot of people were driving at the time. I remember seeing a used XT in 1993 for around $200 or so, which was cheap enough, but state of the art was an 80486 DX2 66...

Today, you can get an entry level sub for Claude Code or Codex for about $20/month... and while that may be really expensive in some parts of the world, it's not nearly as bad as a single state of the art compiler or dev tools in the early 90's over the course of a year or two until the next version came out. Let alone something like an MSDN subscription.

在 HN 中查看
Maxatar 2026/04/14 03:29
本文专门介绍 QBASIC，它与 MS-DOS 以及 Windows 直至 Windows Me 捆绑在一起。 QuickBASIC 是一个独立的应用程序，早于 QBASIC。两者确实有很多相似之处，但它们不属于同一产品线。

Microsoft 开发了多种 BASIC 变体，包括 Altair BASIC、MBASIC、GWBASIC、PDS BASIC，当然还有其中最著名的 Visual Basic。

QBASIC 是其中唯一一个“免费”的，因为它作为操作系统的一部分捆绑在一起，并且从未作为独立产品出售。

This article is specifically about QBASIC, which was bundled with MS-DOS, and by extension Windows up until Windows Me. QuickBASIC is a separate stand-alone application that predates QBASIC. The two certainly shared a lot of similarities, but they were not part of the same product line.

Microsoft developed numerous variations of BASIC from Altair BASIC, MBASIC, GWBASIC, PDS BASIC, and of course the most well known of them all, Visual Basic.

QBASIC was the only of these that was "free" in the sense that it came bundled as part of the operating system, and never sold as a stand-alone product.

在 HN 中查看
themadturk 2026/04/14 04:03
在 QBASIC 可用之前（DOS 5，如果我没记错的话），GWBASIC 就与 MS-DOS 捆绑在一起（免费）。
GWBASIC was bundled (free) with MS-DOS before QBASIC was made available (DOS 5, if I remember right).
在 HN 中查看
queenkjuul 2026/04/14 04:07
每个 80 年代的微型电脑都包含 BASIC，在 XT 上使用 QBASIC 与在 486 上使用 QBASIC 没有什么不同。286 和 386 一直到 90 年代才在办公室广泛使用。

5 年每月 20 美元已经超过 1000 美元，除此之外你还需要硬件。与购买二手电脑和使用图书馆的书籍相比，这并不便宜。

BASIC was included with every 80s micro, and using QBASIC on an XT wouldn't be that different than on a 486. 286s and 386s were in widespread use in offices well into the 90s.

$20/mo for 5 years is over $1000, and you still need hardware on top of that. Vs buying a secondhand computer and using books from the library, it's not cheap.

在 HN 中查看
repelsteeltje 2026/04/14 00:26
与法学硕士相比，我们程序员依赖集中计算资源的时间要长得多。

其一，想象一下无需 Google 搜索即可发现 StackExchange。当然，那些是 自由的，但我不太确定编程是否曾经如此 自由的 正如作者所说。

We programers have been depending on a centralized compute resources for much longer than LLMs.

For one, imagine having to discover StackExchange without Google search. Sure, those were gratis, but I'm not so sure programming was ever as free as the author says.

在 HN 中查看
benterix 2026/04/13 23:30
切线相关：博客作者正在听 LukHash。我记得那些家伙对 C-64 李小龙主题的封面绝对令人惊叹： https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iUHewyaavys
Tangentially related: the author of the blog is listening to LukHash. I remember the guys absolutely stunning cover of C-64 Bruce Lee theme: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iUHewyaavys

stock_toaster 2026/04/14 00:31
哇哦。我怎么还没遇到过这个？！

感谢分享。

Whoah. How have I not run across this yet?!

Thanks for sharing.

在 HN 中查看
polmuz 2026/04/13 23:44
旅行曾经是免费的。你可以步行、跑步或游泳到任何你想去的地方。现在这些汽车和飞机正在毁掉旅行，它们价格昂贵且难以维护。你必须向供应商购买门票，体验与步行完全不同。
Traveling used to be free. You could walk, run or swim anywhere you wanted. Now these cars and airplanes are ruining travel, they are expensive and hard to maintain. You have to buy tickets from vendors and the experience is completely different than walking.
在 HN 中查看
pasquinelli 2026/04/14 00:17
我知道你在开玩笑，但这是一个有趣的类比。考虑一下道路如何常常使步行变得不安全、不可行，甚至公然违法。并考虑汽车文化的外部性。
i know you're joking, but it is an interesting parallel to draw. consider how roads often make walking unsafe, infeasible, or even overtly illegal. and consider the externalities of automotive culture.
在 HN 中查看
xigoi 2026/04/14 00:52
但人们不断地问你，当汽车是未来时，为什么还要骑自行车上班。
This, but with people constantly asking you why you’re cycling to work when cars are the future.
在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:15
你需要追溯到几千年前，才能找到所有旅行都是步行、跑步或游泳的时代。
You need to go back thousands of years to find a time when all traveling was walking, running or swimming.
在 HN 中查看
dtj1123 2026/04/14 00:16
但它是免费的！
But it was free!
在 HN 中查看
boomlinde 2026/04/14 00:41
到目前为止，看起来仍然如此，但我认为我们很快就会有很多复杂且非常稀疏的信息代码，这些代码将成为可供人类阅读的 PITA。

我已经阅读了大量由技术水平较低的开发人员编写的 LLM 生成的代码，并且理解和审查它需要读者对细节的偏执关注，我认为如果这些生成大量代码的工具对您来说似乎是一个不错的选择，那么您可能缺乏这种关注。

非常离题，但我可以发誓 QBasic 包含一个可通过编辑器访问的磁盘文档系统。也许只有以后的版本？

So far it still seems like it still is, but I think we will shortly have a lot of convoluted and very sparsely informational code that will be a PITA to read as a human.

I'm already reading a ton of LLM generated code by less skilled developers and understanding and reviewing it requires a paranoid attention to detail of the reader that I think you probably lack if these tools to generate large chunks of code seems like a good option to you at all.

Very tangential, but I could swear QBasic included an on-disk documentation system accessible from the editor. Maybe only later versions?

在 HN 中查看
purplesyringa 2026/04/14 00:56
> 非常离题，但我可以发誓 QBasic 包含一个可通过编辑器访问的磁盘文档系统。也许只有以后的版本？

也许我的安装不包含它，或者您可能将它与 QuickBASIC 混淆了，QuickBASIC 是一个功能更完整的 IDE，带有编译器（而不仅仅是解释器）。我不太记得了。

> Very tangential, but I could swear QBasic included an on-disk documentation system accessible from the editor. Maybe only later versions?

Perhaps my installation didn't include it, or maybe you're confusing it with QuickBASIC, a more feature-complete IDE with a compiler (instead of just an interpreter). I don't exactly remember.

在 HN 中查看
professoretc 2026/04/14 06:16
我刚刚尝试了 QBasic 1.1，它包含上下文相关帮助：在关键字上按 F1 即可获取其文档。
I just tried QBasic 1.1 and it includes context-sensitive help: press F1 on a keyword to get documentation for it.
在 HN 中查看
alnwlsn 2026/04/13 23:47
我也在大约同一时间学习了 QBASIC 编程，但就我而言，这主要是因为当时所有 90 年代的旧计算机都被扔掉了，所以周围有很多零件可供孩子学习计算机而无需破坏任何昂贵的东西。

当时在互联网上找到适用于这些机器的软件也很容易。我不太确定今天的年轻人是否会像使用昨天的计算机一样容易地学习。

I also learned programming on QBASIC around the same time frame, but in my case it was mostly because all the old 90's computers were getting thrown away at that time, so there were plenty of parts around for a kid to learn about computers without breaking anything expensive.

It was pretty easy back then to find software that would work on those machines on the internet, too. I'm not so sure it would be as easy for young people to learn using yesterday's computers today.

在 HN 中查看
gbacon 2026/04/14 00:13
在使用了 Apple 多年之后，第一次坐下来使用 QBASIC ][ BASIC，我的第一个想法是高兴地“没有行号！”
Sitting down for the first time to QBASIC after years of Apple ][ BASIC, my first thought was a gleeful ‘No line numbers!’
在 HN 中查看
baal80spam 2026/04/14 00:30
然后第二个想法是恐慌：“但是我该如何使用 GOTO？！”
And then the second thought was panic: "But how am I going to use GOTO?!"
在 HN 中查看
z500 2026/04/14 00:38
如果我没记错的话，它甚至附带了一个实用程序来为您删除行号
If I remember correctly, it even came with a utility to remove line numbers for you
在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/13 23:24
它仍然是免费的。没有人强迫任何人使用法学硕士来学习编码。
It still is free. No one is forcing anyone to use LLMs to learn to code.
在 HN 中查看
neko_ranger 2026/04/13 23:37
事实上，当处于“学习”模式时，您可能不应该使用法学硕士。这也是为什么你在学习乘法时不立即跳到计算器的原因。是的，法学硕士比计算更强大，但如果您想花时间，至少您可以手动得出相同/相似的结果
In fact when in "learning" mode you probably shouldn't use an LLM. Same reason why you don't immediately jump to a calculator when learning multiplication. Yes LLMs are more powerful than a calc, but at least you could have arrived at the same/similar result manually if you wanted to spend the time
在 HN 中查看
s08148692 2026/04/13 23:47
当LLM技能成为瓶颈时，您需要使用LLM来学习如何有效地使用LLM工作流程

充分利用 LLM 工具是一项真正的技能，需要像其他任何技能一样进行练习

When LLM skills become the bottleneck, you kind of need to use an LLM to learn how to use LLM workflows effectively

Getting the most out of LLM tooling is a real skill that needs practice just like any other

在 HN 中查看
compass_copium 2026/04/13 23:39
也没有人“强迫”你开车去上班。如果您居住的地方没有良好的公共交通，您可以步行 20 英里。

我的观点是，作者谈论的是免费开发的职业相关技能知识。

如果您在不使用法学硕士的情况下无法培养在面试中具有竞争力的技能，那么您将因社会因素而被迫使用法学硕士。

No one is "forcing" you to drive a car to get to work, either. You could walk 20 miles if you live somewhere without decent public transit.

My view is that the author is talking about having a knowledge of career-relevant skills, developed for free.

If you can't develop the skills to be competitive in an interview without using LLMs, then you are forced by societal factors to use the LLMs.

在 HN 中查看
walljm 2026/04/13 23:51
它是免费开发的吗？他有一台电脑，我猜他是付钱买的。 您可以在本地运行法学硕士。这些最终会迎头赶上。

一直有一条护城河，深度各异。 你有电吗？你有电脑吗？你能负担得起互联网吗？

was it developed for free? he has a computer, which i'm assuming he paid for. and you can run LLMs locally. and those will catch up eventually.

there has always been a moat, with varying levels of depth. do you have electricity? do you have a computer? can you afford internet?

thiagoharry 2026/04/14 00:09 deleted

jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:17
是的，但我们可能会从最好的工具免费的时代过渡到它们绝对不免费的时代。
Yes but we could be transitioning from a time when the best tools are free to a time when they decidedly aren't.
在 HN 中查看
nemoniac 2026/04/14 02:18
那很快。就在前几天，我们有“编程是免费的”

https://idiallo.com/blog/programming-tools-are-free

That was quick. Just the other day we had "Programming is free"

https://idiallo.com/blog/programming-tools-are-free

在 HN 中查看
dakiol 2026/04/14 00:26
我很难理解 HN 中为专有法学硕士提供担保的“黑客”。就像我们有这么多优秀的开源软件，比如 linux、git、postgres、http、tcp/ip 等等，现在我们有这些亿万富翁试图让我们以高昂的价格使用 LLM 进行编码。

我从PG之类的人那里了解到了这一点，但是真正的黑客呢？来吧人们

I struggle to understand the "hackers" in HN vouching for proprietary LLMs. Like we have so much so good open source software that is top notch like linux, git, postgres, http, tcp/ip, and a long etc., and now we have these billionaires trying to make us use LLMs for coding at a hefty price.

I understand it from people like PG and the like, but real hackers? C'mon people

在 HN 中查看
erelong 2026/04/14 00:39
编程并不是真正那么自由，法学硕士只是延续了这种趋势，即给予一些自由感，同时牺牲其他自由

许多人使用锁定的专有软件，甚至 GNU 许可证也因被锁定而受到批评

普遍存在对技术的原始主义批评，表明技术体系如何需要非常严格的全球工业体系

法学硕士之前的时代让我四处寻找常见问题的记录不足的解决方案，并且对可能的解决方案有很大的限制

Programming wasn't really that free and LLMs just continue that trend of giving some feelings of freedom while trading off other freedoms

Lots of people use locked down proprietary softwares and even GNU licenses have been criticized for being locked down

There are primitivist critiques of technology in general that show how technological systems require very restrictive global industrial systems

Pre-LLM eras had me hunting all over for poorly documented solutions to common problems, with vast amounts of limitations on what was possible

在 HN 中查看
headcanon 2026/04/14 00:33
这是我寻求开发本地 LLM 能力的一个重要原因：拥有硬件是一个良好的开端，而且还可以加深对本地边缘模型 SoTA 功能的理解，这样，如果远程模型停止服务，或者至少进行一些风险管理，我们就不会陷入瘫痪。

它并不能解决一般 LLM 依赖性的问题（归根结底，我们必须保持大脑敏锐），但如果我们设置依赖于它的东西，任何基于 LLM 的工作流程都不会突然面临风险。

This is a big part of why I'm looking to develop a local LLM capability: having the hardware is a good start, but also developing the understanding on what the SoTA of local edge models can do, so we're not crippled if remote models stop being served, or at least some risk management.

It doesn't solve the problem of general LLM dependency (at the end of the day we gotta keep our brains sharp), but any LLM-based workflows aren't all of a sudden put at risk if we set up something that depends on it.

在 HN 中查看
mmmlinux 2026/04/14 01:46
编程过去也很简单。
Programming also used to be simple.
在 HN 中查看
phendrenad2 2026/04/14 00:58
这取决于您对“免费”和“编程”的定义。你买得起电脑吗？您能负担得起互联网来访问文档吗？很多人都做不到。同样，对你来说“编程”是什么？ Python 中的你好世界？或者修复 Linux 内核中的驱动程序错误？就完成构建而言，这些硬件要求是天壤之别。
This depends on your definitions of "free" and "programming". Can you afford a PC? Can you afford internet to access documentation? A lot of people can't. Likewise, what is "programming" to you? Hello World in Python? Or fixing a driver bug in the Linux kernel? Those are worlds apart in terms of hardware requirements just to complete the build.
在 HN 中查看
DeathArrow 2026/04/14 00:57
生活中没有什么是免费的。
Nothing in life is free.
在 HN 中查看
david38 2026/04/13 23:40
编程比以往任何时候都更自由、更快、更共享，并且得到更多的企业赞助。

您认为免费找到其他人编写的高质量文档和软件包总是那么容易吗？

Programming is freer, faster, more shared, and has more corporate sponsorship than ever before.

You think it was always this easy to find high quality docs and packages written by others for free?

在 HN 中查看
h05sz487b 2026/04/14 00:04
是的，这是非历史的。过去您必须为编译器付费，更不用说可用的 IDE 了。
Yep, this is ahistoric. You used to have to pay for your compiler, not to mention a useable IDE.
在 HN 中查看
PeterWhittaker 2026/04/14 00:17
我已经这样做了近 40 年，而且从来不需要为此付费。

现在，如果您找不到 gcc，并且 vi（以及后来的 vim）或 emacs 都无法使用，那么我们就不要去那里了。

工具也在不断变得更好。现在我有 clang 和 gcc，还有很多酷炫的 vim 插件可供选择。

我仍然为好的硬件付费，但多亏了 Linus 和他的同类，我几乎不需要再这样做了。

I've been doing this almost 40 years and have never had to pay for either.

Now, if you don't find gcc and neither of vi (and later vim) or emacs usable, well, let's not go there.

And the tools, they just keep getting better. Now I have both clang and gcc, and so many wayy-cool vim plugins to choose from.

I still pay for good hardware, but thanks to Linus and his ilk, I barely need to do that anymore.

在 HN 中查看
jimbokun 2026/04/14 00:19
这篇文章涵盖了这一点。
The article covers this.
在 HN 中查看
kibwen 2026/04/14 00:12
这不是历史性的。回到过去开发人员工具成本过高（更不用说运行它所需的硬件）的糟糕时代将是一种社会倒退。想象一下 2026 年需要订阅才能使用编程语言，你会被笑出房间。这就是法学硕士提供商试图将我们拉回的世界。
It's not ahistoric. Going back to the bad old days of forbiddingly high costs to developer tooling (not to mention the hardware needed to run it) would be a societal regression. Imagine needing a subscription to use a programming language in 2026, you'd be laughed out of the room. That's the world that the LLM providers are trying to drag us back to.
在 HN 中查看
tmseidman 2026/04/14 00:31
LLM 不是编程。
LLMs aren't programming.
在 HN 中查看
